name: Louisiana Division of Administration (OTS)
tagline: Louisiana's central management and IT hub: the Division of Administration runs the state budget, the Office of State Procurement, and — through the Office of Technology Services (OTS) — the consolidated technology shared by every executive-branch agency.
overview: The Louisiana Division of Administration (DOA) is the management and finance arm of the executive branch — it drafts the state budget, manages facilities and capital outlay, and houses two functions that matter most to a technology seller: the Office of Technology Services (OTS), the state's consolidated central IT organization, and the Office of State Procurement (OSP), which owns statewide contracts and cooperative purchasing. OTS was created by the Legislature in 2014 to consolidate IT out of the agencies and into a single shared-services provider; it now supports roughly 40,000 executive-branch users and runs the state's data centers, network, security operations, and enterprise applications. Critically, OTS is an ancillary agency that recovers its costs through a fee-for-service model — its operating budget flows through inter-agency transfers and self-generated funds rather than a direct general-fund appropriation, so its scale shows up in the services it bills back to agencies rather than a headline line item. For a Dell SLG seller, DOA/OTS is the central buying and standards-setting center for state-government technology in Louisiana, sitting alongside OSP's procurement vehicles as the path to most executive-branch IT spend.

budget.intro: OTS is an ancillary, fee-for-service organization — it does not receive a direct general-fund appropriation but recovers operating costs through inter-agency transfers and self-generated funds. Its leverage is the volume of consolidated IT it bills back to agencies, set against Louisiana's overall state budget, which the Division of Administration itself drafts.

stats.label: OTS operating scale
stats.note: Widely cited fee-for-service operating budget; recovered via inter-agency transfers, not a direct appropriation

stats.label: State budget (FY 2026-27)
stats.note: Gov. Landry's ~$47B 'standstill' executive budget; HB1 advanced spring 2026

stats.label: Executive-branch users
stats.note: Population OTS serves across consolidated state agencies

notes: Because OTS recovers costs from agencies, technology spend is funded by the agencies it serves — confirm which entity owns the budget line for a given program before forecasting.
The FY 2026-27 budget is a 'standstill' plan holding most programs at current-year levels; net state spending is down from FY26 as one-time federal funds roll off.
OTS publishes a multi-year Strategic Plan (FY 2026-27 through FY 2030-31) that frames modernization, security, and AI priorities — a useful public roadmap for sequencing the motion.

initiatives.name: AI governance & acceptable-use policy
initiatives.summary: OTS issued a statewide Artificial Intelligence Acceptable Use Policy effective Sept. 29, 2025, setting guidelines for responsible AI use by state agencies and employees. AI is an active, governed track — lead with governed, secure AI infrastructure rather than ungated tooling.

initiatives.name: Security operations modernization
initiatives.summary: OTS modernized its SOC as an early adopter of an AI-driven security platform (Palo Alto Cortex XSIAM) and participates in LSU-led whole-of-state cyber collaboration over LONI. Security is a consolidated, statewide function run from OTS.

initiatives.name: Legacy modernization & data-center consolidation
initiatives.summary: OTS continues refreshing legacy hardware/software and runs consolidated state data centers under its shared-services model — an ongoing infrastructure-refresh and hybrid-cloud track across agencies.

initiatives.name: FY27–FY31 Strategic Plan execution
initiatives.summary: OTS's multi-year strategic plan commits to delivering modern, secure, cost-effective services to executive agencies — the public roadmap to align infrastructure, security, and AI offers against.

procurement.intro: State-government technology buying in Louisiana runs through the Division of Administration's Office of State Procurement (OSP), with OTS acting as the centralized customer for IT acquisition under the consolidation statute. Confirm where Dell's coverage sits — statewide contracts, cooperative vehicles, and national cooperatives are all in play.

vehicles.name: OSP statewide contracts
vehicles.detail: The Office of State Procurement competes and manages statewide contracts for goods, services, and IT that executive-branch agencies buy from — the primary route for most state IT purchases.

vehicles.name: Cooperative purchasing
vehicles.detail: Louisiana's cooperative procurement clause lets participating entities (local government, etc.) buy off statewide contracts; OSP also recognizes national cooperatives such as NASPO ValuePoint for IT and cloud.

vehicles.name: OTS as centralized IT customer
vehicles.detail: Under the 2014 consolidation, OTS is the sole centralized customer for acquisition, billing, and recordkeeping of state technology services — a key buying center alongside OSP.

vehicles.name: LaPAC / LaGov supplier portal
vehicles.detail: Solicitations post on LaPAC (the Louisiana Procurement and Contract Network); vendors register and receive bid notices via the LaGov supplier self-registration portal.

powerStructure.name: Taylor Barras
powerStructure.title: Commissioner of Administration
powerStructure.note: Gov. Landry's top budget official and head of DOA; appointed 2024, continuing into 2026. Owns the state budget and the division that houses OTS and OSP.

powerStructure.name: Evelina Broussard
powerStructure.title: State CIO, Office of Technology Services
powerStructure.note: Appointed CIO in March 2025 after ~three decades in private-sector IT (Atmos Energy). Top technology relationship for the state-agency motion.

powerStructure.name: Chase Hymel
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), OTS
powerStructure.note: Longtime OTS enterprise security architect; became state CISO after Dustin Glover moved to the Chief Cyber Officer role. Owns consolidated security operations, the modernized SOC, and enterprise security standards across the executive-branch agencies OTS supports.

powerStructure.name: Amy Vincent
powerStructure.title: Director, Office of State Procurement
powerStructure.note: Owns statewide contracts, cooperative purchasing, and procurement policy — the path onto the vehicles agencies buy through.

powerStructure.name: Dustin Glover
powerStructure.title: Chief Cyber Officer (state)
powerStructure.note: Louisiana's first-ever Chief Cyber Officer and a former state CISO; coordinates whole-of-state cyber risk and incident response across OTS, the Military Department, GOHSEP, and State Police — relevant for whole-of-state cyber programs.

news.title: Louisiana enacts cyber & data-governance laws; OTS named administrator of new Statewide Data Exchange Compact
news.impact: The 2026 session (adjourned June 1) produced three notable measures: SB 233 makes OTS the administrator of a voluntary interagency Statewide Data Exchange Compact (standardized data-sharing agreements + advisory committee); SB 75 pushes national cybersecurity standards onto local governments that seek state incident help (non-compliant entities must reimburse response costs); and SB 386, the Louisiana Data Privacy Act, takes effect Jan. 1, 2027. OTS's data-governance and whole-of-state cyber mandate is expanding — align secure data-exchange, identity, and cyber-compliance offers.

news.title: Louisiana House advances ~$47B 'standstill' FY 2026-27 budget (HB1)
news.impact: Flat funding pressures discretionary IT spend toward cost-recovery and consolidation — frame ROI and shared-services economics.

news.title: Meta's Richland Parish data center tops $875M in Louisiana contracts in year one
news.impact: Meta's ~$27B AI data center (operational target ~2030) is reshaping Louisiana's power, workforce, and digital-economy narrative — context for statewide AI-infrastructure and data-center momentum.

news.title: OTS issues statewide AI Acceptable Use Policy (effective Sept. 29, 2025)
news.impact: Establishes governed AI use across state agencies — sell into a governed framework, not ungated tools.

news.title: Evelina Broussard named State CIO at OTS
news.impact: New private-sector-veteran CIO sets technology direction for the consolidated state-IT organization — re-anchor the top relationship.

dellActions: Map Dell's coverage on OSP statewide contracts and national cooperatives (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint) — confirm the compliant path for compute, storage, and client devices into executive-branch agencies.
Engage OTS as the centralized IT customer and standards-setter; tie infrastructure and client offers to the consolidated shared-services and data-center model.
Lead with governed, secure AI infrastructure aligned to OTS's Sept. 2025 AI Acceptable Use Policy and its modernized, AI-driven SOC.
Anchor the relationship to State CIO Evelina Broussard and OTS security leadership; reference the FY27-FY31 Strategic Plan to sequence modernization.
Use the Meta Richland Parish data-center buildout as macro context for Louisiana's AI/data-center momentum, while keeping the account scoped to state-government IT spend.
